verses.commentary: Jacob heard that Laban's sons were saying, Jacob has taken everything our father owned and has gained all this wealth from what belonged to our father. Laban's sons.
Jacob noticed. Hostility develops.
To your relatives.
Private meeting away from Laban's house.
Contrast: Laban hostile, God faithful.
You know I worked with all my strength.
Laban's repeated deception failed. God has not allowed him to harm Jacob. Divine protection thwarts human schemes.
God overruled Laban's manipulation.
God has taken. Given to me. Justice served.
Divine revelation.
The angel of God speaks in the dream. Jacob, here I am (hineni, הִנֵּנִי). Ready to obey.
God sees injustice. The breeding wasn't magic but divine intervention.
I am the God of Bethel. God calls Jacob back to his vow (28:18-22). Leave at once. Return to your homeland. Clear divine command.
Do we have any share? They recognize disinheritance.
Sold us (through bride price). Used up payment.
The wives side with Jacob against their father.
Jacob acts. Flight preparation.
Drove livestock.
Rachel stole household gods (teraphim, תְּרָפִים). Why? Inheritance claim? Sentimental? Idolatry? The text doesn't explain but condemns it (verse 30).
Jacob deceived Laban by not telling him. Literally stole his heart. Secret departure necessary for escape.
Fled with everything. Crossed the Euphrates. Headed for Gilead. Decisive escape from twenty years of exploitation.
Third day. Discovery delayed.
Laban pursued. Seven days. Hot pursuit.
Be careful. Warning to Laban.
Both parties camped. Tense standoff.
Laban's accusation masks his real concern: loss of control.
Unlikely given his character.
But God warned him (verse 24).
Laban admits divine restraint.
Laban's real concern emerges: why steal my gods? The household gods (teraphim) represent family authority and inheritance rights. His daughters are gone, but his gods?
Thought you would take daughters by force.
Jacob unknowingly curses Rachel (she dies in childbirth, 35:16-19). Public search.
Laban searches systematically: Jacob's tent, Leah's tent, servants' tents. Nothing found. Rachel's deception works.
Rachel sitting on the idols. Laban searches everywhere. The deception succeeds through unlikely means.
I cannot stand. Having my period.
Jacob's anger erupts. What is my crime? How have I wronged you? Innocence regarding the theft emboldens him. Twenty years of tension explodes.
Jacob demands public judgment. Innocence emboldens him.
Twenty years and I haven't eaten your rams. Jacob's defense: faithful service despite abuse.
And you demanded payment from me for whatever was stolen by day or night. Jacob absorbed losses Laban should have borne.
Heat by day, cold by night, sleep fled from my eyes. The hardship of shepherding for a cheat.
Twenty years total: fourteen for your daughters, six for flocks. God's protection prevented Laban from cheating me completely.
Had the God of my father, the Fear of Isaac, not been with me, you would have sent me away empty. But God saw my hardship and rebuked you. Divine vindication.
Laban claims everything: daughters, grandchildren, flocks. Everything is mine. But what can I do? God has thwarted him. Impotent fury of the powerless.
Let's make a covenant between us as witness. Mutual distrust formalized.
Set as pillar. Like Bethel (28:18).
Gather stones, pile them in a heap. Covenant meal on the cairn. Physical memorial.
Jacob called it Galeed (Hebrew: גַּלְעֵד, heap of witness).
Heap is witness.
Mizpah (מִצְפָּה), watchtower. May the LORD keep watch between us when apart. Not a blessing but mutual suspicion: God must watch because we don't trust each other.
God is witness. Conditional curse.
This heap and pillar are witnesses. Physical markers of distrust.
You won't cross to harm me.
May the God of Abraham, God of Nahor, God of Terah judge between us. Solemn oath invoking ancestral deities.
Offered sacrifice, spent the night. Covenant sealed through sacrifice and fellowship meal.
Early morning, Laban blessed them and left. Final parting. The relationship ends.
